@graph.name: Mercedes‑Benz SLS AMG GT Engine Era Map
Mercedes‑Benz SLS AMG GT Engine Codes

mainEntity.name: How much does a Mercedes‑Benz SLS AMG GT engine replacement cost in the UK?

acceptedAnswer.text: A reconditioned SLS AMG GT (M159) engine typically costs £7,000–£15,000 supply only depending on condition and specification. Replacement engines for AMG models can cost £10,000–£20,000 depending on condition and warranty. A blown engine on a luxury car can cost £9,000–£13,000 from a dealer. Fitting adds £1,200–£2,500 at independent specialist rates. Use our calculator for a figure specific to your car.

mainEntity.name: Is the Mercedes‑Benz SLS AMG GT engine reliable?

acceptedAnswer.text: The M159 is the ultimate iteration of AMG's naturally aspirated V8, but it has well‑documented issues. Valve train noise and tapping on cold start due to 'bleed down' of valve buckets is a common complaint. Camshaft adjuster wear can cause rattling noises. Head gasket failures have been reported on some engines. Oil consumption of up to 0.8 litres per 1,000 km is considered normal. Some owners report trouble‑free ownership with proper maintenance, while others report 'questionable engine reliability'.

mainEntity.name: What are the common problems with the Mercedes‑Benz SLS AMG GT?

acceptedAnswer.text: Valve train noise and cold‑start tapping (valve bucket bleed‑down), camshaft adjuster wear (rattling from timing area), head gasket failure, oil consumption (0.8L per 1,000 km considered normal), AMG SPEEDSHIFT DCT 7‑speed transmission issues (shifting delays, grinding, rough shifts), and electrical/battery issues. See the Era Map above for detailed advice.

mainEntity.name: Is it worth replacing a Mercedes‑Benz SLS AMG GT engine, or should I scrap the car?

acceptedAnswer.text: Depends on vehicle value. Against a well‑kept SLS AMG GT (values £120,000–£250,000+ depending on condition and mileage), a £8,000–£10,000 reconditioned engine is 4–8% of the vehicle's value — worth it. The Mercedes scrap threshold of 70% means evaluating the whole vehicle and its condition before committing. Given the SLS AMG GT's status as a future classic, engine replacement is almost always worth it for a solid example.

mainEntity.name: How do I find my Mercedes‑Benz SLS AMG GT engine code?

acceptedAnswer.text: The engine code M159.980 is listed on the VIN plate and vehicle documentation. The SLS AMG GT (2013–2014) uses the 6.2‑litre M159 naturally aspirated V8 producing 583 bhp. The M159 is a heavily reworked version of the M156, featuring a dry‑sump lubrication system, revised intake, upgraded valvetrain, and tubular steel exhaust manifolds. Tell us your registration and we'll confirm for free.

mainEntity.name: Can I repair the M159 valvetrain noise, or does it require engine replacement?

acceptedAnswer.text: Valve train noise is usually repairable without replacing the engine — if caught early. Affected bucket tappets can be replaced individually, but if mechanically damaged, all bucket tappets and camshafts on both cylinder banks must be replaced. Upgraded M159 valve buckets with anti‑friction coating are available as a permanent fix. At the first sign of tapping or rattling, book an inspection immediately.

replacementCosts.h2: Mercedes‑Benz SLS AMG GT Engine Replacement Costs

rows.bestFor: Budget priority; finding a good donor M159 is challenging

replacementCosts.figuresNote: Figures above reflect the SLS AMG GT M159 era (2013–2014). Replacement engines for AMG models can cost £10,000–£20,000 depending on condition and warranty. A blown engine on a luxury car can cost £9,000–£13,000 from a dealer.
replacementCosts.labourEstimate: Fitting an SLS AMG GT engine typically takes 14–20 hours at £85–£130/hr (independent) or £140–£200/hr (London/SE dealer). The M159's dry‑sump system, front‑mid engine placement and rear‑transaxle layout add significant complexity. [PLACEHOLDER — confirm vs cost sheet]

cta.label: → Compare SLS AMG GT engine prices from UK specialists

problems.title: Valve Train Noise & Cold‑Start Tapping (M159)
problems.symptoms: Loud tapping or clattering noise from the engine on cold start, lasting until the engine reaches operating temperature; noise reduces or disappears when warm.
problems.typicalMileage: Any mileage — can occur on low‑mileage examples
problems.replacementCost: £7,000–£15,000 (if valvetrain wear leads to engine failure)【PLACEHOLDER】

urgency.label: Monitor
urgency.text: book inspection soon; can indicate accelerated valvetrain wear

problems.recommendation: Many M156 valve buckets are susceptible to "bleed down," resulting in excessive valve lash until the engine warms up. This excessive valve lash is a significant factor in accelerated valvetrain wear. The M159 engine found in the SLS features valve buckets of an improved design, but issues can still occur. A loud tapping noise during cold starts is the classic symptom. If you experience this, have the valve buckets and camshafts inspected. Weistec offers upgraded M159 valve buckets with an anti‑friction coating and an enhanced hydraulic system to address this issue. If wear is detected, affected bucket tappets should be replaced; if mechanically damaged, all bucket tappets and camshafts on both cylinder banks should be replaced.

problems.title: Camshaft Adjuster Wear (M159)
problems.symptoms: Rattling noise from the engine timing area after extended vehicle standstill or on cold start; metallic clattering from the front of the engine.
problems.typicalMileage: Any mileage — can occur on low‑mileage examples
problems.repairCost: £1,500–£4,000 (camshaft adjuster replacement — timing set required on both banks)【PLACEHOLDER】
problems.replacementCost: £7,000–£15,000 (if adjuster failure leads to engine damage)【PLACEHOLDER】

urgency.label: Immediate
urgency.text: if you hear rattling, book an inspection today

problems.recommendation: The M159 can suffer from rattling noises from one or several camshaft adjusters. Mercedes‑Benz issued a technical service bulletin (LI05.20‑P‑063356) for M156/M159 engines addressing this issue. The remedy involves checking the adjusters and actuating them per Xentry, then replacing affected adjuster(s) if necessary. Critically, even if only one camshaft adjuster is replaced, it is absolutely essential to set the timing on both cylinder banks. At the first sign of rattling from the timing area, have the camshaft adjusters inspected immediately.

problems.title: Head Gasket Failure (M159)
problems.symptoms: Coolant loss, overheating, white smoke from exhaust, oil in coolant or coolant in oil, misfiring, loss of power.
problems.replacementCost: £7,000–£15,000 (if head is cracked or engine is damaged)【PLACEHOLDER】

urgency.label: Immediate
urgency.text: if you notice coolant loss or overheating, stop driving immediately

problems.recommendation: Head gasket failure is a known issue on M156/M159 engines. Early M156 engines (engine numbers below approximately 60,000) used the older head bolt design, which can fail. If you own an SLS AMG GT with an earlier engine number, consider proactive head bolt replacement with updated ARP studs. If you experience any coolant loss, overheating, or white smoke, have the engine inspected immediately. This is a potentially catastrophic failure that can require a complete engine replacement.

problems.title: Oil Consumption (M159)
problems.symptoms: Low oil warning light, needing to top up oil frequently (0.8 litres per 1,000 km or more).
problems.repairCost: £150–£300 (regular oil top‑ups and monitoring) — £1,000–£4,000+ (if oil consumption is excessive and requires investigation)【PLACEHOLDER】
problems.replacementCost: £7,000–£15,000 (if oil starvation leads to engine damage)【PLACEHOLDER】

urgency.label: Monitor
urgency.text: book inspection soon; monitor oil levels regularly

problems.recommendation: Mercedes‑Benz states that oil consumption of up to 0.8 litres per 1,000 km is normal for the M159 engine. Some vehicles may have more oil consumption or "blow‑by" than others. Check the oil level regularly and check the oil separator every 3,000 miles to ensure it is not filled. If consumption exceeds 0.8 litres per 1,000 km, have the engine inspected for potential issues.

problems.title: AMG SPEEDSHIFT DCT 7‑Speed Transmission Issues
problems.symptoms: Shifting delays, grinding or jumping during acceleration, rough shifting, transmission overheating warnings, vehicle shaking.
problems.repairCost: £1,000–£3,500 (transmission service, mechatronic repair, or clutch replacement)【PLACEHOLDER】

urgency.label: Monitor
urgency.text: book inspection soon if shifting issues develop

problems.recommendation: The AMG SPEEDSHIFT DCT 7‑speed sports transmission, while praised for its fast shifts, can develop issues. Problems can show up as shifting delays, grinding or jumping during acceleration, or the car shaking on the road. Regular transmission fluid services are essential. If you experience any of these symptoms, have the transmission inspected by a specialist with AMG DCT experience.

problems.title: Electrical & Battery Issues
problems.symptoms: Fault codes triggered by battery replacement, electrical resets, loss of ECU memory, instrument cluster warnings.
problems.repairCost: £200–£800 (battery replacement, software reset, or module reprogramming)【PLACEHOLDER】

urgency.label: Monitor
urgency.text: book inspection soon; can affect vehicle operation

problems.recommendation: SLS AMG GT users often face fault codes triggered by battery replacement and related electrical resets. After replacing a dead battery, fault codes may appear due to lost ECU memory or sensor misalignment. Starting by clearing the codes with an OBD‑II scanner can resolve many issues. If problems persist, have the vehicle diagnosed by a Mercedes specialist.

repairBuyOrReplace.h2: Mercedes‑Benz SLS AMG GT — Repair, Buy, or Replace?

rows.whenItMakesSense: Upgraded M159 valve buckets are a permanent fix
Catch it early — far cheaper than engine replacement
Proactive head bolt upgrade recommended; complete engine replacement may be required if severe
Regular servicing and mechatronic repair is cost‑effective
Software resets and battery replacement are straightforward
M159 failures are rare but catastrophic when they occur

buyingChecks.title: Buying This SLS AMG GT? Check These First
buyIf: * Full service history with regular oil changes using AMG‑approved oil
* No cold‑start tapping or rattling — valve train and camshaft adjusters should be healthy
* No coolant loss or overheating history — head gasket should be intact
* Transmission serviced (DCT fluid changes every 20,000–30,000 miles)
* Extended warranty in place — these cars are expensive to repair out of warranty
avoidIf: * No proof of regular maintenance — the M159 is unforgiving of neglect
* Cold‑start tapping or rattling — valvetrain or camshaft adjuster work is imminent
* Any coolant loss or overheating — head gasket failure may be imminent
* Transmission issues — DCT repairs are expensive
* Any engine warning light without diagnosis — the M159 can hide expensive faults
* "Cheap" examples — the purchase price is the least of your worries

repairBuyOrReplace.closingVerdict: The Mercedes‑Benz SLS AMG GT is one of the most iconic supercars of the modern era — a gullwing‑doored, front‑mid‑engined V8 masterpiece that pays homage to the 300 SL Gullwing while delivering devastating performance. The M159 6.2‑litre naturally aspirated V8 is the ultimate evolution of AMG's celebrated M156 engine, producing 583 bhp and a soundtrack to die for. With only 350 Final Edition examples produced, the SLS AMG GT is a future classic.

cta.label: → Compare SLS AMG GT engine prices before you buy

buyingChecklist.intro: Before you buy a used Mercedes‑Benz SLS AMG GT (or before you commit to an engine replacement), check these:
items: * Cold Start — Listen for tapping or rattling from the engine (valve train bleed‑down or camshaft adjuster warning)
* Service History — Regular oil changes with AMG‑approved oil are absolutely essential
* Valve Train — Any history of valve bucket or camshaft work? (upgraded M159 buckets are a plus)
* Camshaft Adjusters — Any rattling from the timing area? (camshaft adjuster wear is a known issue)
* Coolant Level — Check cold and hot; any loss indicates potential head gasket issues
* Head Bolt Work — Has the car had head bolts replaced with updated ARP studs? (recommended)
* Transmission — Any shifting delays, grinding, or rough shifts? (DCT issues are common)
* Oil Level — Check dipstick; excessive consumption indicates potential issues
* Engine Warning Light — Have codes been read? Valvetrain, timing, or coolant codes are most common

engineCodes.matchNote: This table's rows exactly match Section 1B's Era Map — the same engine code, years, and generation coverage.
